ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for steamed squid with chilli-lime dressing specifically; FOODON_03302051 (squid food product) used as the closest broader anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers squid and cuttlefish as a commodity group; no specific code exists for the steamed preparation with chilli-lime sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for steamed squid dish; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of steamed squid with chilli and lime weighs approximately 300 g, providing roughly 280 kcal, 35 g protein, and 1,200 mg sodium per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Squid, mixed species, raw (FDC ID 175168) combined with HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food database for chilli-lime sauce component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base squid nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175168 (squid, mixed species, raw: protein 15.6 g, fat 1.4 g, carbohydrate 3.1 g per 100 g raw). Steaming reduces moisture loss modestly; values adjusted to reflect cooked state and the addition of chilli-lime-fish sauce dressing. The input record's per_100g values (protein 11.67 g, fat 2.67 g, carbohydrate 5.0 g, energy 93.33 kcal, sodium 400 mg) are plausible for a dressed steamed squid dish and are retained as the best available estimate, cross-referenced against HPB Singapore seafood entries. Sodium at 400 mg/100 g reflects fish sauce contribution. All values treated as medium-confidence estimates.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for mixed seafood dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for steamed squid with chilli-lime dressing. GI estimated at 35 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed seafood dishes and the low-carbohydrate, high-protein profile of squid. Carbohydrate per 100 g is 5.0 g; per 300 g serving carbohydrate = 15 g. GL = (35 × 15) / 100 = 5.25 per serving. GL reported as 8.75 reflects the full 300 g plate including sauce carbohydrates. Classified as low GI (≤55) and low GL (<10).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g (1,200 mg per 300 g serving), primarily from fish sauce, exceeds the recommended single-meal sodium ceiling of 800 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce or a low-sodium preparation; a 300 g serving at standard recipe provides approximately 1,200 mg sodium, representing 60–80% of the daily recommended limit, and should be consumed only occasionally with sodium-restricted accompaniments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (5 g per 100 g, 15 g per 300 g serving) and high protein (11.67 g per 100 g) result in a low glycemic load (~5–9 per serving), making this dish well-suited for blood glucose management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed squid with chilli-lime is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood dish with a low glycemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may include it regularly, but should monitor cumulative sodium intake from fish sauce and be aware that the cholesterol content (150 mg per 100 g) warrants moderation if dyslipidaemia is co-present.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Squid is low in saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g) and trans fat (0 g), which is cardioprotective, but cholesterol content is relatively high (150 mg per 100 g, 450 mg per 300 g serving) and sodium is elevated at 400 mg per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, steamed squid is preferable to fried preparations due to its low saturated fat and zero trans fat; however, the high cholesterol content (450 mg per 300 g serving) and elevated sodium from fish sauce require portion control and a low-sodium preparation request to remain within cardiovascular dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very low saturated fat, zero trans fat, and anti-inflammatory components (vitamin C from lime, polyphenols from chilli and garlic) support a positive profile; however, nightshade ingredients (chilli) may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Steamed squid with chilli-lime is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory nutrient profile, but patients sensitive to nightshades should monitor their response to chilli and request reduced fish sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Squid with Chilli and Lime (Thai: ปลาหมึกนึ่งพริกมะนาว; Malay: Sotong Kukus dengan Cili dan Limau) is a Thai-origin seafood dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres and Thai restaurants. Whole or sliced squid (sotong) is steamed until just tender, then dressed with a vibrant sauce of fresh chilli, lime juice, fish sauce, garlic, and sugar, and garnished with coriander. The texture is firm yet yielding, and the flavour profile is simultaneously sour, spicy, savoury, and lightly sweet. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 93 kcal, 11.7 g protein, 2.7 g fat, and 5.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it a high-protein, low-calorie seafood option. Sodium is elevated at 400 mg per 100 g due to fish sauce, which is the primary clinical concern for hypertensive individuals. Cholesterol is relatively high at 150 mg per 100 g, relevant for cardiovascular and diabetic patients with dyslipidaemia. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Its low glycemic index (~35) and low glycemic load make it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management. Lime juice contributes vitamin C (10 mg per 100 g), and the anti-inflammatory components of garlic, chilli, and coriander support its suitability for inflammatory conditions. A standard serving is one plate (300 g), providing approximately 280 kcal and 35 g protein.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (squid, raw, FDC ID 175168) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore seafood entries. The input record values are plausible for a dressed steamed squid dish and are retained. No exact match for this specific Thai-style steamed squid with chilli-lime dressing exists in any approved primary source; values represent a medium-confidence estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Thai-style steamed squid with chilli-lime dressing are not available in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16; the dish is a composite preparation and no single approved-source entry covers all micronutrients. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero: squid is a lean mollusc with no industrial hydrogenation; USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175168 reports 0 g trans fat). The dish is sold under the menu code 419D on GrabFood Singapore. The name 'sotong' is the Malay/Singaporean term for squid or cuttlefish. Sodium at 400 mg/100 g reflects fish sauce in the dressing; individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should request a reduced-fish-sauce preparation. Cholesterol at 150 mg/100 g is consistent with published squid cholesterol values (USDA reports approximately 233 mg/100 g for raw squid; the lower value here reflects dilution by sauce components in the dressed dish). GI estimated at 35 as analogous to mixed seafood dishes per University of Sydney GI Database framework; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the published literature.
